does: This paper systematically investigates knowledge distillation during language model pretraining by constructing various teacher-student configurations with different architecture sizes and training budgets. The authors find that contrary to conventional wisdom, weak or undertrained teachers can effectively improve larger student models through distillation when combined with appropriate loss weighting between language modeling and distillation objectives. Critically, they demonstrate that stronger teachers do not monotonically improve student performance—increasing teacher capacity or training tokens can plateau or even degrade distillation benefits. The work shows distillation particularly enhances generalization on out-of-distribution and downstream tasks rather than in-domain performance, and provides empirical evidence that effective distillation does not require the typical strong teacher assumption that dominates the field.
connects: This work extends the classical knowledge distillation literature, which has long operated under the assumption that teacher quality directly determines student quality, into the specific context of LLM pretraining where this relationship had not been thoroughly examined. It builds on recent findings questioning standard distillation intuitions while engaging with the broader body of work on efficient LLM training and transfer learning. The paper relates to research on loss function combinations during pretraining and on understanding what properties make models suitable teachers. It also connects to work examining generalization phenomena in language models and how different training objectives affect downstream performance, drawing on insights from both classical distillation theory and modern scaling law studies.
enables: These findings open new directions for efficient LLM pretraining by suggesting practitioners need not invest in expensive strong teacher models, potentially reducing computational costs during training. The results prompt future investigation into what specific properties of teachers matter for distillation—perhaps beyond raw capacity—and how to optimally balance multiple training objectives. The work enables more principled approaches to curriculum learning and multi-task pretraining by questioning when additional model strength translates to pedagogical value. It also motivates deeper theoretical understanding of why weaker teachers sometimes suffice, which could inform architecture design and training procedures. Finally, the emphasis on generalization over in-domain fitting could redirect focus toward distillation objectives that prioritize robustness and transfer, impacting how foundation models are developed for diverse downstream applications.
